title: The Part That Can't Stop Achieving
researcherTopic: the body keeps the score, trauma and the brain
metaDescription: For those who can't stop achieving, healing from trauma means learning to rest. This article explores the roots of over-functioning and the path to embodied rest.
imageDescription: A pair of hands gently cradle a luminous, golden thread, weaving it into a complex, beautiful pattern. The background is soft and warm, suggesting a process of gentle repair and integration. The overall feeling is one of hope and healing.
htmlContent:
Bessel van der Kolk’s work reminds us that the body keeps the score, that trauma is not a story that is told, but a visceral experience held in the tissues and the nervous system. For many who have endured developmental trauma, this score is kept by a part of the self that cannot stop achieving, a relentless internal engine that believes its constant motion is the only thing keeping everything from falling apart. This part doesn’t know rest; it only knows momentum. It’s the straight-A student, the over-performing employee, the friend who is always available, the one who holds it all together while quietly fracturing on the inside. It has learned that its value is conditional, its safety purchased through relentless effort, and its existence justified only by its output. The exhaustion is a constant hum beneath the surface, a bone-deep weariness that no amount of sleep can touch, because the work it is doing is not in the world, but in the psyche.

The Architecture of Over-Functioning

The achieving part is not a flaw in the system; it is a brilliant, if painful, adaptation to a system that was once deeply flawed. It is a manager, in the language of Internal Family Systems, that took on its role in a chaotic or unsafe environment where its hyper-vigilance and productivity were necessary for survival. This over-functioning is a survival strategy that became a personality, a set of behaviors that once protected us from external threat and have now become an internal tyrant. It operates on the implicit, pre-verbal belief that to stop moving is to be annihilated, to rest is to be found wanting, to be still is to be exposed to a danger that feels both imminent and ancient. In my years of working in this territory, I’ve seen this pattern appearance as a deep-seated inability to receive help, a profound discomfort with stillness, and a constant, low-grade anxiety that only abates when the next task is identified and conquered. The body, meanwhile, continues to keep the score in the form of chronic tension, digestive issues, and a perpetually activated sympathetic nervous system. We might think we are getting away with it, but the body is an honest accountant. It sends invoices in the form of migraines, autoimmune flares, and the quiet desperation of burnout. The work is not to dismantle this part, but to understand its origins, to hear its story, and to gently, patiently, begin to unburden it from a job it was never meant to hold for a lifetime. We can begin by exploring the myth of the safe childhood to understand the roots of these deeply ingrained patterns.

Every resistance is information. The question is whether you're willing to read it.

The Body as a Perpetual Motion Machine

For the part that can’t stop achieving, the body is not a home but a vehicle, a machine to be optimized and pushed, its signals of distress often ignored or overridden in the service of the next goal. This is not a conscious choice but a deeply ingrained somatic pattern, a way of being in the world that is organized around the avoidance of feeling. The constant doing, the endless striving, the perpetual motion—it all serves to keep the deeper, more vulnerable feelings at bay. It’s a way to outrun the ghosts of the past, to stay one step ahead of the emptiness or the terror that threatens to surface in moments of quiet. The achieving part believes that if it can just build a life that is successful enough, impressive enough, and busy enough, then the underlying pain of the trauma will be rendered irrelevant. But the body has its own logic, a wisdom that operates independently of our conscious will. It remembers the neglect, the chaos, the feeling of being unsafe, and it will continue to send up flares until its messages are heard. These flares—the anxiety, the chronic pain, the fatigue—are not the problem; they are the solution, the body’s attempt to communicate what the mind has disavowed. The path forward involves learning to listen to this somatic language, to befriend the sensations we have been so desperate to avoid. It is a process of turning toward the body, not as a project to be fixed, but as a source of profound intelligence. This journey often involves understanding the principles of somatic experiencing and how they can help us reconnect with our bodies in a safe and titrated way.

The Critic and the Achiever: A Co-Dependent Relationship

Behind every relentless achiever, there is often an equally relentless inner critic. These two parts exist in a painful, co-dependent relationship, a feedback loop that generates a constant state of internal pressure. The critic sets an impossibly high bar, whispering that we are not good enough, that we are lazy, that we are failing. The achieving part, in a desperate attempt to silence the critic and finally feel a moment of peace, springs into action. It strives, it perfects, it over-delivers, hoping to finally earn the critic’s approval. For a fleeting moment, upon the completion of a task or the receipt of external praise, the critic might quiet down. But the relief is always temporary. The critic’s job is not to be satisfied; its job is to protect us from the perceived dangers of complacency and failure. And so, it moves the goalposts. This cycle is exhausting and is at the heart of so much of the suffering associated with complex trauma. The achiever is running a race it can never win, fueled by the fear of the critic’s judgment, which is itself a stand-in for the judgment of original caregivers. Healing this dynamic requires us to step out of the loop altogether. It involves recognizing that both the critic and the achiever are wounded parts of ourselves, doing their best to keep us safe. It requires us to bring compassion to both parts, to acknowledge the critic’s fear and the achiever’s exhaustion. It is a process of learning to source our sense of worth from a deeper place, a place that is not contingent on performance or perfection. It is about finding the voice of self-compassion that can speak to both the critic and the achiever, and offer them the rest they so desperately need.

Silence is not the absence of noise. It's the presence of attention.

The High Price of Conditional Worth

The relentless achiever is often praised by the outside world, rewarded for its productivity, its reliability, its seemingly endless capacity to get things done. But this external validation comes at a high internal price: the perpetuation of the belief that our worth is conditional. We learn, at a cellular level, that we are loved for what we do, not for who we are. This creates a profound sense of instability, a constant need to prove our value, to earn our place in the world. The achieving part is locked in a tragic feedback loop, its efforts to secure safety and connection only reinforcing the core wound of not being enough. It is a lonely and exhausting existence, one that is often marked by a sense of being an imposter, a fraud who is just one mistake away from being exposed. A client once described this as feeling like a beautifully decorated house with no foundation. The work of healing involves building that foundation, cultivating a sense of unconditional self-worth that is not dependent on external validation. It is a slow and tender process of reparenting the parts of ourselves that have been so deeply wounded by the experience of conditional love. It requires us to challenge the deeply held belief that we must perform to be worthy, and to begin to experiment with the radical act of simply being. This is not an easy task, as it often brings us into contact with the very feelings the achieving part has been working so hard to avoid. But it is the only way to truly come home to ourselves, to find a sense of peace that is not contingent on the next accomplishment.

Unraveling the Knots of Identity

For many, the achieving part is not just a part; it has become an identity. We are the "successful one," the "responsible one," the "one who has it all together." This identification makes the process of unburdening this part particularly challenging, as it can feel like we are losing a core aspect of who we are. The fear is that without our achievements, without our productivity, we will be nothing. This is the great illusion that trauma perpetuates: that our survival strategies are the sum total of our being. The truth is that we are so much more than our adaptations. We are the awareness that can witness the achieving part, the compassion that can hold its pain, and the wisdom that can guide it toward a new way of being. The work is to gently and patiently begin to disentangle our sense of self from the roles we have played, to create a space between the observer and the observed. It is a process of expanding our identity to include all of our parts, not just the ones that are socially acceptable or productive. At this juncture, the real work of integration begins, as we learn to embrace the full spectrum of our humanity—the messy, the vulnerable, the parts of ourselves that we have long since exiled. It is a journey from a life that is driven by fear to a life that is guided by a deeper sense of purpose and meaning. We can learn more about this process by exploring the concept of Internal Family Systems (IFS) and how it can help us heal our inner family.

Information without integration is just intellectual hoarding.

The Slow Road to Embodied Rest

The antidote to relentless achieving is not collapse or laziness; it is embodied rest. This is not the same as sleep or distraction. It is a state of being in which the nervous system can downshift from a state of chronic activation to a state of ventral vagal safety, where the constant hum of hyper-vigilance can begin to quiet, and where we can experience a felt sense of safety in our own skin. For the achieving part, this can feel terrifying, a surrender that it equates with death. It has been on high alert for so long that the absence of threat can be more dysregulating than its presence. This is why the journey toward rest must be slow and titrated, a gentle process of expanding the window of tolerance for stillness and ease. It might begin with just a few moments of conscious breathing, a brief pause between tasks to feel the feet on the floor, or a deliberate choice to do nothing for five minutes. These small acts of rebellion against the tyranny of productivity begin to create new neural pathways, to teach the nervous system that it is possible to be still and be safe. It is a process of unlearning the old patterns of survival and learning a new language of being, one that is based on presence, attunement, and self-compassion. This is not a linear process, and there will be times when the achieving part will reassert its dominance. But with each conscious choice to return to rest, we strengthen the muscle of our own liberation. We can find support in this process by connecting with resources that teach us about the power of conscious rest and its role in healing.

Where Healing Actually Lives

Ultimately, healing the part that can’t stop achieving is a journey of returning. It is a return to the body, a return to the present moment, and a return to a more authentic and integrated sense of self. It is a process of reclaiming the parts of ourselves that we have abandoned in the name of survival, and of weaving them back into the tapestry of our being. This is not a quick fix or a one-time event, but a lifelong practice of tending to our inner world with kindness, curiosity, and courage. It is about learning to mother and father ourselves in the ways we were not mothered or fathered, to provide the safety, the attunement, and the unconditional love that we have always longed for. As we do this work, the achieving part begins to soften, to release its grip, and to trust that it no longer has to carry the full weight of our survival. It can begin to take on a new role, one that is based on its inherent strengths of creativity, focus, and determination, but that is no longer driven by fear. It can become a source of joyful and purposeful action in the world, rather than a defense against the ghosts of the past. This is the promise of healing: not that the trauma will disappear, but that it will no longer define us. It will become a part of our story, but not the whole story. And in its place, we will find a deeper, more resilient, and more expansive sense of who we are. We can explore this journey of returning further by reading about the return to the self and what it means to come home.

Patience is not passive. It's the active practice of allowing something to unfold at its own pace.

Enough reading about healing. Enough collecting frameworks and bookmarking articles. At some point the research becomes another form of avoidance. You know this. Your body knows this. The only question left is whether today is the day you stop preparing and start practicing.

Frequently Asked Questions

Why do I feel so anxious when I try to relax?

For a nervous system wired for hyper-vigilance, the absence of threat can be more dysregulating than its presence. The achieving part has learned to equate stillness with danger, so when you try to relax, it can trigger a cascade of anxiety and fear. This is a normal part of the process, and it is important to approach it with gentleness and curiosity, slowly expanding your capacity for rest rather than forcing it. It is a sign that your body is starting to touch into the stored activation that the business has been keeping at bay.

How can I start to unburden my achieving part?

The first step is to bring awareness to it, to notice when it is active and what triggers it. You can then begin to dialogue with it, to acknowledge its hard work and to reassure it that you are here to help. This is not about getting rid of the part, but about building a relationship with it, one that is based on trust and collaboration rather than conflict. You might even ask it what it is afraid would happen if it were to rest for a moment.

What if I lose my ambition and drive?

This is a common fear, but it is based on a misunderstanding of what it means to heal. The goal is not to eliminate ambition, but to transform it from a fear-based survival strategy into a source of joyful and purposeful action. As you heal, you will find that your drive becomes more aligned with your authentic desires and a sense of contribution, rather than being a reaction to past trauma and a need to prove your worth. The energy that was once used for hyper-vigilance becomes available for creation.

The journey of healing the achieving part is not about becoming less, but about becoming more whole. It is a tender and courageous process of reclaiming all the parts of yourself, and of learning to live from a place of deep and abiding self-love, a place where your being is more important than your doing.
